name: Dehesas de Guadix - Villanueva - Pedro Martínez Loop
name_original: Del Río Fardes por la Acequia del Toril — circular desde las Dehesas de Guadix
description: Moderate 36.5-mile road cycling from Dehesas de Guadix through Granada Geopark's Badlands, Río Fardes, and Acequia del Toril.
region_intro: Road cycling around Dehesas De Guadix features a distinctive landscape of arid badlands and hilly desert terrain, providing both challenging and scenic routes. The region is characterized by eroded geological formations and the presence of rivers such as the Río Fardes and Río Guadiana Menor. Cyclists can experience varied elevation profiles, often with distant views of the Sierra Nevada mountains. This area, part of the Geopark of Granada, offers a unique backdrop for road cycling. Best road cycling routes in Dehesas De Guadix The most popular road cycling route is Negratín Reservoir Loop via Pozo Alcón and Hinojares, a 39.1 miles (62.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 17 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the Negratín Reservoir and surrounding arid landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Dehesas de Guadix - Villanueva - Pedro Martínez Loop, a moderate 36.5 miles (58.8 km) path. This route traverses through local villages and offers a mix of open plains and rolling hills. Local road cyclists also love the Alamedilla and Puente del Hacho Loop, a 49.2 miles (79.2 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 36 minutes. This difficult route includes significant elevation changes and panoramic vistas. Road cycling around Dehesas De Guadix is defined by arid badlands, hilly desert landscapes, and distant Sierra Nevada views. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes with substantial climbs. The routes in Dehesas De Guadix are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 90 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Dehesas De Guadix's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level for road cycling in Dehesas De Guadix?
A: Road cycling in Dehesas De Guadix offers a mix of challenges. While there are 3 moderate routes, the majority of the 9 available routes, specifically 6 of them, are considered difficult. The region's hilly desert landscapes and badlands provide undulating terrain with significant elevation gains, making for engaging and often challenging rides suitable for those with a good fitness level.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes for beginners in Dehesas De Guadix?
A: Yes, there are moderate options available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987052699/negratin-reservoir-loop-via-pozo-alcon-and-hinojares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Negratín Reservoir Loop via Pozo Alcón and Hinojares&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 62.9 km route that takes around 3 hours 17 minutes. Another moderate choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1350397557/dehesas-de-guadix-villanueva-pedro-martinez-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dehesas de Guadix - Villanueva - Pedro Martínez Loop&lt;/a&gt;, covering 58.8 km.

Q: What kind of landscapes and natural features can I expect to see while road cycling?
A: You'll experience a distinctive landscape of arid badlands and hilly desert terrain, often referred to as 'eroded landscapes,' which creates a visually stunning backdrop. Routes traverse through the Gorafe Desert area, and you can enjoy breathtaking distant views of the majestic Sierra Nevada mountains. The region is also part of the Geopark of Granada, indicating rich geological formations.

Q: What natural landmarks or viewpoints can I visit along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer access to stunning natural sights. You can explore areas like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4101272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorafe Desert&lt;/a&gt;, known for its unique formations. For panoramic vistas, consider routes that pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5520220" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puntal de Don Diego Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or offer views of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5459236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Coloraos Badlands&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2073244" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Acequia del Toril&lt;/a&gt; is another notable natural monument.

Q: Are there any cultural attractions or unique sights along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in unique cultural elements. You might encounter traditional cave houses (casas cueva), a defining characteristic of the wider Guadix area. The remains of the prehistoric town of 'Terrera del Reloj' in Dehesas De Guadix also offer a glimpse into ancient history, often accessible via challenging paths that can be incorporated into cycling routes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Dehesas De Guadix?
A: Given the arid and desert-like characteristics of Dehesas De Guadix, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling. Temperatures are milder, and the light can be particularly beautiful. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon rides preferable, while winters are cooler but often still suitable for cycling.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many of the routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987052699/negratin-reservoir-loop-via-pozo-alcon-and-hinojares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Negratín Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1350397557/dehesas-de-guadix-villanueva-pedro-martinez-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dehesas de Guadix - Villanueva - Pedro Martínez Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345026799/alamedilla-and-puente-del-hacho-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alamedilla and Puente del Hacho Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Dehesas De Guadix?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the unique and dramatic scenery, including the arid badlands and distant Sierra Nevada views, as well as the varied and challenging terrain that makes for engaging rides.

Q: Are there any particularly long or challenging road cycling routes for advanced riders?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38149160" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fardes River – Cave Quarter, Guadix loop from Alicún de Ortega&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route spanning over 107 km with significant elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345026799/alamedilla-and-puente-del-hacho-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alamedilla and Puente del Hacho Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 79.2 km long and features substantial elevation changes and panoramic vistas.

Q: What is the total number of road cycling routes available in Dehesas De Guadix?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes available in Dehesas De Guadix, catering to various skill levels from moderate to difficult.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through different villages or towns?
A: Yes, several routes are designed to connect local villages, offering a mix of open plains and rolling hills as you pass through. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1350397557/dehesas-de-guadix-villanueva-pedro-martinez-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dehesas de Guadix - Villanueva - Pedro Martínez Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which traverses through various local communities.
tips: Impressive road that passes by the Río Fardes and through this canyon that has been created by the same river that at times, even seems far away. It's worth getting there!

The Acequia del Toril is a porous limestone aqueduct, more than 1.5 km long and more than 10 meters high at some points. Its natural construction over millennia has been used since prehistoric times to supply a settlement from one of the eight nearby hot springs. This meandering geological formation, with walls up to three meters thick, remains an impressive testament to natural engineering and human use of water.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a moderate road ride. While it features mostly well-paved surfaces, be aware that some segments in this region might be unpaved or incorporate gravel tracks, especially given the 'Badlands' terrain of the Granada Geopark. A good level of fitness is recommended, and if you prefer to explore potential rougher sections, a mountain bike or e-bike might offer more versatility.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers dramatic scenery through the unique 'Badlands' of the Granada Geopark. You'll cycle past the Fardes River and experience the remarkable Acequia del Toril, a natural aqueduct formed by thermal springs. The area also features ancient megalithic dolmens and petroglyphs, offering a glimpse into its rich history.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the arid 'Badlands' environment, the best times to cycle this route are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are generally milder and more comfortable for cycling. Summers can be very hot, and winters can bring colder temperatures, though the region generally experiences many sunny days.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as 'moderate' with an elevation gain of over 800 meters and a distance of nearly 59 kilometers. It requires a good level of fitness and some experience with road cycling. Beginners might find it challenging due to the distance and elevation, as well as potentially varied terrain.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the cycling route from Dehesas de Guadix?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route are not detailed, Dehesas de Guadix serves as the starting point. You should be able to find suitable parking within or on the outskirts of the town. It's always advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while there are no explicit restrictions against dogs, it's generally not recommended to bring dogs on long road cycling tours due to safety concerns with traffic and the physical demands on the animal. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure it can safely travel in a bike trailer and that you have plenty of water for both of you, especially in the arid environment.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle in the Granada Geopark?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need special permits or to pay an entrance fee to cycle this route within the Granada UNESCO Global Geopark. The Geopark is an open landscape, and access to public roads and trails is generally free. However, always respect local regulations, private property, and environmental guidelines.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with routes such as Ruta del Mencal, Acequia del Toril y dólmenes de Alicún, El Camino Espiritual del Sur, and Camino de Pedro Martínez a los Barros, among others. These intersections are generally brief, as this route primarily follows its own distinct path.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or accommodation near the route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Dehesas de Guadix, where you can find basic amenities, including cafes and potentially accommodation. For more options, the nearby town of Alicún de las Torres also offers services. It's recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks, as facilities may be sparse along the majority of the route itself.

faqs.question: What makes the Acequia del Toril so unique?
faqs.answer: The Acequia del Toril is a truly unique natural wonder. It's a natural aqueduct formed over centuries by limestone deposits from warm thermal springs. This process has created a towering, wall-like structure, up to 15 meters high, through which water flows, creating a ribbon of rich vegetation that contrasts dramatically with the surrounding arid 'Badlands' landscape.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for completing this route is approximately 2 hours and 15 minutes. However, this is an average for racebike cycling and can vary significantly based on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery or take breaks.
